table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: Biography -- Medicine, music, and baseball -- Playing piano for silent movies -- First exposure to jazz/Moving to New York -- 1930's jazz favorites/Walter Page/Condon's scene -- \u0022'Round midnight' is a hit with me\u0022/Zess Confrey tunes -- Playing in a big band in Nova Scotia/Hearing Casa loma -- Medical career: learning Neurosurgery as a medic at Normandy -- Awe of musicians/Caring for the patient, not the disease -- Jazz friends."]

label: Stanley Kay interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 18, 2001
table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: Biography -- Always drawn to drummers, especially Buddy Rich -- Depression-era radio/Assistant to Buddy Rich/Playing on \u0022How much is that doggie in the window?\u0022 -- Failed to graduate high school because he was spending all his time at the Paramount Theater -- Post-War changes in the music business/Describes Buddy Rich's best qualities -- Buddy Rich stories : playing with a broken arm/Couldn't play his own drum book -- Buddy Rich's practical jokes : throwing a pie at Mel Torme -- Anecdotes about diva, his all-female big band -- Music today : young players haven't listened to anything prior to Coltrane -- Wouldn't allow talking in his club/Diva accused of racism -- Worked with George Steinbrenner -- Conclusion : more on diva."]

table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: Biography -- Jazz opportunities from living in New York -- Classical and jazz training/Won lessons from Teddy Wilson in a contest -- Touring Europe with Benny Goodman/European audiences and jazz -- Making a living doing a wide variety of work -- \u0022Piano music should sound as if the performer is improvising it\u0022 -- Arranging : learning the craft/Poor readers -- Arranging for Count Basie/Tempos and keys -- Working on the Arthur Godfrey show : \u0022Godfrey had fallen in love with jazz\u0022 -- Work with early synthesizers -- Musical interests/Always liked ragtime and stride styles -- Impressions of Charlie Parker -- Film work -- Pros and cons of synthesizers -- Early \u0022odd jobs\u0022 on Beat the Clock and accompanying silent movies -- Joe Williams : \u0022the greatest jazz singer there ever was\u0022 -- Touring in Russia with the New York Repertory Company -- Listening suggestions for aspiring jazz pianists -- Musical evolution/Jazz to rock transition -- Conclusion."]

label: Jackie Cain and Roy Kral interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 22, 1998
table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: Biography -- \u0022Love at first note\u0022/First jobs; joining forces -- Picking up the vocalese sound/Joining Ventura -- Writing Ventura book in three weeks -- Vocalese demonstration/Big band album -- Vocalese arrangements/Writing scat phonetically -- Records from the Ventura years -- Studs Terkel -- Marriage and short-lived group with cello -- Musical influences/Cultural distillation -- Jazz education/Jazz in Japan -- Reality/On the road with a baby -- Gig with Ventura band in New Jersey -- Alec Wilder/Move to Blue Angel Club in New York -- Appearing on Tonight show after being recognized working at a grocery store -- Getting a job in Vegas/At the Sands -- Incorporating rock 'n roll/Electric instruments -- Finding a home in New York -- Moving into the lucrative jingle business/Forming their own production company -- Finding a permanent home/Losing a daughter -- Performing again: \u0022music saved us\u0022 -- New standards being written -- Album recorded after daughter's death -- Recording: commercial pressure -- On the road: taking care of health -- Early influences/Role of radio -- Developing vocal style/Sarah Vaughan -- Memories: Marian McPartland -- Conclusion."]

table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: Biography -- About the Preservation Hall Band/Musical stagnation in New Orleans/Family music background -- Racial opportunities : greater opportunities for \u0022White Creoles\u0022 -- More on family/Musical beginnings and eventual job replacing Dizzy Gillespie for Cab Calloway -- First Dixieland gig : as \u0022weakest link\u0022 made bandleader for the night and called the songs -- Early gigs while still in business school -- Getting into Lionel Hampton Band : \u0022Lionel paid weekly, very weakly\u0022 -- Touring with various groups and his own band -- Demonstrates New Orleans feel/Describes Clifford Brown lick in his playing -- A mature sound : Cannonball Adderley and Clark Terry -- Lack of blues in jazz today -- Suggested listening list for trumpet students -- Louis Armstrong : \u0022that guy paid dues that no one had to pay after him.\u0022"]

label: Donn Andre interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, June 6, 2009
table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: First jazz group at Hamilton College/Black and white jazz -- The 52nd Street scene -- More about Hamilton and musical upbringing -- Lasting effect from Hamilton College -- Status nationally of Dixieland -- More about Hamilton/Military service -- Playing Dixieland correctly/The Alumni Jazz Band; \u0022Donn Andre is a 1958 graduate of Hamilton College, a banjo player and lifelong fan and performer of Dixieland jazz. He is a member, with other Hamilton alums, of the Alumni All-Stars Jazz Band, one of the longest running ensembles in the country. label: Manny Albam interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998
table_of_contents: ["Table of Contents: Biography -- Current workload: \u0022getting hot again\u0022 -- Writing and working with strings -- Musical background: first heard classical, then heard Bix Beiderbecke -- Pee Wee Russell: \u0022had that essence of jazz\u0022 -- Hearing the band as a writer from the Bari Chair -- Decision to give up playing and going on the road to write -- Story about Milt and Mona Hinton at Giants games -- Long-term musical memory -- First arrangements/Learning to write with no score sheet from Fletcher Henderson -- Learning arranging from Budd Johnson and Dizzy Gillespie -- Effects of ASCAP: splitting writing royalties with leaders -- About standards: usually didn't use the verse -- Developing personal style: turning down project to write charts like Nelson Riddle's -- About writing for Basie and other leaders/Writing for pay -- Writing in the car on the way to the session -- Love of recording sessions: players turn soundless score into music -- About the blues is everybody's business -- Arranging vs. orchestration -- Writing with or without a piano -- About West Side Story record -- Writing chamber/classical music/Big compliment from Bernstein: \u0022Would you ever write anything for my big band?\u0022 --Listening choices: prefers live to recorded jazz -- Story about his mother meeting Coleman Hawkins and Benny Carter -- About Ben Webster: loyalty to the Ellington Band -- Living on 52nd street/Funny jingle scene story -- About writing for rock acts -- Problem of bass amplification -- Writing for Broadway: \u0022a sense of drama\u0022 in the dynamics -- About the soul of the city: descriptive music -- Conclusion: Funny quote about short sketches"]
